title: Real-world clinical and economic evaluation of remote programming for deep brain stimulation in Parkinson's disease
methods: Demographically Adjusted Parkinson Care Inequity Index (DPII)
Bass diffusion model for technology adoption
data_sources: Global Burden of Disease Study 2021 (IHME / GHDx)
National Bureau of Statistics of China
City-level population projections (figshare, Cai & Zhang et al.)
Resource and Environmental Science Data Platform (China basemap)

data_scope.geographic_coverage: mainland China (all provinces and prefecture-level cities)

core_conclusion: Across 20,383 Parkinson's disease patients and 42,163 remote programming sessions at 439 hospitals in China (2012–2024), RP for DBS is clinically equivalent to onsite programming while reducing the demographically adjusted healthcare access inequality index (DPII) 30% faster. Economic modelling projects annual benefits of ¥1.09 billion (reduced domestic medical tourism) and ¥8.15 billion (labour savings), with cumulative gains of ¥115–270 billion by 2050 — concentrated disproportionately among low-income, remote, and advanced-disease patients whose cost savings are 2–10 times greater.
why_it_matters: Largest real-world interventional telemedicine dataset nationally; clinical equivalence confirmed at scale; novel DPII equity metric; quantified equity-economy double dividend; long-horizon SSP-scenario projections translatable to policy; generalizable blueprint for device-mediated chronic-disease telemanagement worldwide.
why_nature: Population-scale evidence with direct policy impact; novel integrated clinical-population-economics framework; equity-at-scale narrative aligning with Nature Health's health justice mandate; China's scale makes findings transferable to other rapidly ageing societies globally.
added_value_to_my_research: DPII disease-burden weighting maps directly onto R2SFCA demand side — enabling a 2SFCA-based fine-grained spatial accessibility analysis of RP-capable DBS centres
Mobile phone / connectivity data to quantify digital-divide barriers to RP adoption, connecting telehealth to my digital divide research theme
Explainable ML (XGBoost + SHAP) on the 3,048-patient follow-up cohort to predict individual RP benefit, applying my spatial-prediction methods to interventional telemedicine
Spatiotemporal KDE and network community detection on RP session geography to map diffusion waves from seed cities — a health geography application of my core spatiotemporal methods
Connecting MRIO economic shock framework to geospatial patient-hospital flows for a spatially explicit health-economy coupling model extending my space-human systems agenda
